name: Devadaru
scientificName: Cedrus deodara (Roxb. ex D. Don) G. Don
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Powerful Vata and Kapha Hara (pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas). Due to its Tikta (bitter) and Katu (pungent) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Shwayathu-hara' (edema-relieving) and 'Vata-hara' (Vata-pacifying) therapies, clearing the cold and heavy qualities of Kapha. It may increase Pitta if used in excess.
Guna (Qualities): Laghu (light), Snigdha (unctuous/oily) - specifically the wood oil; the wood is more Ruksha (dry).

Karma (Actions): Shwayathu-hara (edema-reliever), Vata-hara (Vata-pacifying), Jwarahara (antipyretic), Kasahara (cough-reliever), Krimighna (anthelminthic), and Amavata-hara (rheumatoid arthritis-reliever).

API/AFI Adherence: The heartwood and essential oil are the official medicinal parts as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is a primary ingredient in iconic classical formulations like Devadarvadyarishta, Devadarvadi Kwatha, and various medicated oils for joint pain.
Specific Indications: Used in Amavata (rheumatoid arthritis), Shotha (generalized edema), Jwara (chronic fever), Kasa (cough), Shwasa (asthma), and Pinasa (rhinitis/sinusitis).
usageForm: Heartwood powder (Churna), Essential oil (Taila), Heartwood decoction (Kwatha), Heartwood paste (Kalka).
mainMedicinalUses: Expert Vata and Joint Support: Devadaru is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all types of chronic Vata and joint disorders. Its Snigdha (oily) and Ushna (heating) nature helps in nourishing the 'Asthi-Majja' (bone-marrow) system and relieving deep-seated joint inflammation. WHO-aligned research supports its anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects.

Edema and Fluid Management: Historically known as the 'Shwayathu-hara' (remedy for swelling). It is used to reduce systemic edema (dropsy) and localized inflammation by promoting the healthy flow of fluids through its Vata-Kapha pacifying property.

Respiratory Health Specialist: Acts as a powerful agent for managing asthma, bronchitis, and chronic cough. It helps in liquefying and expelling thick mucus from the lungs through its Tikta-Katu (bitter-pungent) and Ushna (heating) action.

Chronic Fever and Toxins: Effectively acts as an antipyretic (Jwarahara) and diaphoretic, clearing the 'Ama' (metabolic toxins) responsible for chronic and inflammatory fevers. It is traditionally used in 'Vishama Jwara' (intermittent fever) management.

Skin Health and Wound Healing: Frequently used for its antifungal and antiseptic properties. The essential oil (Deodar oil) is traditionally applied topically to treat chronic skin diseases, dandruff, and localized dermatitis. It promotes aseptic healing of wounds.

Urinary and Kidney Care: As a mild diuretic (Mutra-janana), it helps in relieving urinary tract infections and promoting healthy urine flow by clearing Kapha-related obstructions in the bladder and kidneys.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in sesquiterpenoids (himalacarene, himachalol, deodarone), taxifolin (a potent flavonoid), and diversos essential oils that provide its multifaceted medicinal benefits, particularly its potent anti-oxidant and anti-microbial properties.
plantProfile: Unique History: A highly sacred and majestic tree of the Himalayas, often called the 'Tree of Gods' (Deva-Daru). Mentioned in ancient Ayurvedic texts for its unmatched 'Shwayathu-hara' (swelling-destroying) providing properties. It is culturally spiritually associated with 'Sattva' (purity and clarity).

Physical Characteristics: A large evergreen coniferous tree growing up to 40-50 meters in height, with a majestic crown and dark-grey, rough bark. It features narrow, needle-like leaves and attractive, woody cones. Its heartwood is intensely fragrant and durable due to high essential oil content. It is found primarily in the Western Himalayas (altitudes 1200m to 3000m).

Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Sesquiterpenoids (Himachalene, Deodarone, Atlantone), Flavonoids (Taxifolin), and Steroids. The presence of himachalol and deodarone is crucial for its anti-inflammatory and neuro-relaxant action.

Strict Standardizations: The heartwood is standardized in API based on macroscopic features (fragrant, yellowish-brown core). Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<1%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>1%), and water-soluble extractive (>1.5%). High essential oil yield (2-3%) is a marker of quality.
preparationMethods: Heartwood Decoction (Kwatha): 20g of heartwood boiled in 160ml water reduced to 40ml for chronic Vata pain and edema. Take twice daily before meals.
Heartwood Powder (Churna): 3-6g of fine powder taken twice daily with milk or honey (for cough) or warm water (for general vitality).
Essential Oil (Taila): The wood oil is traditionally applied topically (mixed with sesame oil) to treat joint pain, localized inflammation, and skin diseases.
Devadarvadyarishta: 15-30ml of fermented extract taken twice daily after meals for managing chronic fever and joint disorders.
Heartwood Paste (Kalka): Ground wood with water into a fine paste; applied directly to the forehead for fever and headache or to localized swellings to reduce heat.

dosages.Children: Generally safe. 1-2 ml of decoction or 125-250 mg of powder mixed with honey twice daily for cooling during infections.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 3-6 grams; Decoction: 50-100 ml; Oil: 1-2 drops (internal, with precautions), divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Ideal for age-related Vata issues and maintaining joint health. Dosage: 3g of powder daily with milk at bedtime.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Generally very safe. However, individuals with high Pitta (burning sensation, hyperacidity) should use it in moderation as it is Ushna (heating). Avoid during pregnancy as it is a potent diaphoretic.
GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb with a history of use spanning millennia across various traditional systems (Ayurveda, Unani, and Himachali medicine) without documented toxicity.
Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-diuretic or sedative medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in WHO monographs.
Adverse Effects: None documented at traditional therapeutic levels. Excessive use in very sensitive individuals may rarely cause mild skin warmth or stomach burning.
